ai_root_cause: Timeout config reduced from 60s to 30s in recent deployment
ground_truth: Timeout config reduced from 60s to 30s in deploy d4f2a

ai_root_cause: Null check removed in JWT validation logic
Connection pool size not increased with traffic spike
ground_truth: Pool size (50) insufficient for 3x traffic increase

ai_root_cause: Blocking HTTP call introduced in consumer thread
ground_truth: Blocking call to delivery-tracker-api in consumer

ai_root_cause: DNS race condition in automation system deleted all DynamoDB endpoint IPs
ground_truth: DNS planner/enactor race condition deleted endpoint IPs (AWS Oct 2025 real incident)
Network partition between search-service and ES cluster

ai_root_cause: Deployment commit-52f8e1940 configured inventory-service HTTP client timeout to 30000ms (in InventoryClient.java / PaymentServiceConfig.java), but inventory-service P95 latency is 28–32 seconds, causing 71% of payment requests to hit SocketTimeoutException when inventory-service latency spiked into its normal upper percentile range.

Deployment commit-52f8e1940 configured inventory-service HTTP client timeout at 30000ms (in PaymentServiceConfig.java/InventoryClient.java), which is below inventory-service's normal P95 latency of 28–32s, causing 71% of payment requests to fail with SocketTimeoutException when inventory-service latency spiked during peak traffic.

Deployment commit-52f8e1940 configured the inventory-service HTTP client timeout to 30000ms, which is below inventory-service's normal P95 latency of 28-32s, causing widespread SocketTimeoutExceptions once inventory-service latency naturally fluctuated above the threshold.

Deployment v3.2.0 increased Tomcat max threads from 50 to 200 and removed request queuing in OrderService.java, but the HikariCP database connection pool size was left at 10, causing up to 200 concurrent threads to contend for 10 DB connections and exhaust the pool.

Deployment v2.5.3 (commit 9b2e7a33f) introduced a synchronous blocking HTTP call to delivery-tracker-api inside EmailSenderService.sendWithRetry(), which executes on the Kafka consumer thread with a 45-second timeout, causing the consumer to stall on every message and producing catastrophic lag on the order.confirmed topic.

Deployment v1.9.1 (commit f4c8a211d) removed the null-safety guard on the token parameter in UserService.validateToken() at line 88, causing a NullPointerException when clients (notably mobile) send requests with a null token value.

Deployment v1.9.1 (commit f4c8a211d) removed the null-safety guard (`token == null`) from UserService.validateToken() at line 88, causing a NullPointerException when mobile or unauthenticated clients send requests with a null token value.

The inventory-service HTTP client timeout is configured at 30000ms (should be ≥60000ms per runbook RB-1042), and inventory-service P95 latency of 28900ms causes ~71% of requests to exceed this threshold during normal-to-elevated load conditions.

AWS-side DNS infrastructure failure: an automated DNS planner/enactor race condition in AWS's internal DNS management system deleted all A-record IP addresses for the dynamodb.us-east-1.amazonaws.com endpoint, causing UnknownHostException for all DynamoDB operations in us-east-1.
Deployment 25 minutes ago changed inventory.http.timeout.ms from 60000 to 30000 ms. inventory-service P95 latency is 28-32s under load, exceeding the new timeout.
